title: Unit C-2 Self-Check (3 questions, for confirming understanding -- no pass/fail record is saved)

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between generative AI and copyright?
choices: As long as it was generated by AI, it does not constitute copyright infringement even if it closely resembles an existing work
Generated output can resemble an existing work, and entering someone else's copyrighted material can raise reproduction issues, so checking is needed
Copyright concerns belong only to for-profit companies and need not be considered when used at a hospital or university
Every generative AI service has identical terms of use, so once read there is no need to check again
questions.explanation: Generative AI output can include material, derived from its training data, that closely resembles an existing copyrighted work, so checking is needed before publishing or distributing it. In addition, entering someone else's copyrighted work, such as a textbook or paper, to produce material for distribution can also raise issues of reproduction or adaptation. The idea that AI-generated content never infringes copyright (option 1) is incorrect, and use at a hospital or university is not exempt from copyright considerations either (option 3). Terms of use differ across services and are also subject to revision (option 4).

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between generative AI output and bias?
choices: Proceed on the premise that output always contains bias from the training data, correcting for it via the prompt
Subscribing to a paid version of generative AI guarantees that its output will always be completely free of bias
For questions specifically within the medical field, biases inherited from the training data never occur at all
If you're worried about bias, you can feel reassured by asking the same AI whether its answer is biased and hearing "no"
questions.explanation: Because generative AI output reflects statistical patterns in its training data, it must be handled on the premise that it always contains some bias -- involving language, region, gender, era, and so on. Bias you notice can be corrected, for example, by explicitly specifying conditions in the prompt. Using a paid version does not guarantee the absence of bias (option 2), and the medical field is no exception (option 3). Asking the AI itself is not a guarantee, since that answer is generated by the same underlying mechanism (option 4).

questions.q: Which of the following best describes verification before using generative AI output in clinical work?
choices: If the AI's output reads fluently and includes very specific, concrete detail, human verification can simply be skipped entirely
It is enough to have the AI itself perform the verification and report back that its own output is accurate
Human verification is mandatory before use, checked from the perspectives of fact-checking, audience fit, and harm potential
Once an AI service has passed verification one time, all of its later output can be used without further checking
questions.explanation: Human verification is mandatory before using generative AI output in clinical work. The three perspectives are fact-checking -- cross-referencing with primary sources such as the package insert or guidelines; fit for the intended audience -- whether it suits the intended reader or the conditions at your own institution; and harm potential -- whether there is anything that could cause misunderstanding or give inappropriate advice. Fluency or specificity is not evidence of correctness (option 1), and having the AI check itself is not a substitute for human verification (option 2). Verification is needed for each individual output; treating a service as having passed once and for all is dangerous (option 4).
